title: Hashmaps
description: Hash maps provide O(1) lookups — the most frequently tested data structure in interviews. Master TypeScript's Map<K,V>, Record types, and object types to solve frequency counting, two-sum, and grouping problems.

sections.title: Map<K,V> basics
sections.explanation: TypeScript's `Map<K,V>` is the go-to hash map for interviews. It supports any key type (not just strings), preserves insertion order, and has clean methods: `set`, `get`, `has`, `delete`, `size`. Always prefer Map over plain objects when keys can be numbers or complex types.

exercise.prompt: Create a `Map<string, number>` called `scores`. Set "alice" to 95, "bob" to 87, "charlie" to 92. Print alice's score, whether "dave" exists, and the map size.
exercise.starterCode:
const scores: Map<string, number> = new Map()

scores.set("alice", ___)
scores.set("bob", ___)
scores.set("charlie", ___)

console.log(scores.get("alice"))
console.log(scores.has("dave"))
console.log(scores.size)

validation.message: Use Map methods: set, get, has

exercise.hint: Set the scores as numbers: 95, 87, 92. Map.has returns false for missing keys.

sections.title: Frequency counting pattern
sections.explanation: The most common Map pattern in interviews: count occurrences. For each element, get its current count (default 0) and increment. This solves anagram checks, most-frequent-element, and character-frequency problems.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `wordCount` that takes a string array and returns a Map<string, number> counting occurrences. Test with ["go", "stop", "go", "go", "stop"]. Print the count for "go" and "stop".

validation.message: go appears 3 times, stop appears 2 times
Use the nullish coalescing pattern for frequency counting

exercise.hint: Default to 0 when the key doesn't exist using the ?? (nullish coalescing) operator, then add 1.

sections.title: Record<K,V> and object types
sections.explanation: For string-keyed maps, TypeScript offers `Record<string, number>` as a typed object literal. This is lighter than Map and works well when you know all keys are strings. Access with bracket or dot notation. Use `Object.keys()`, `Object.values()`, and `Object.entries()` to iterate.

exercise.prompt: Create a `Record<string, number>` called `prices` with apple: 1.5, banana: 0.75, cherry: 2.0. Add "date" with price 3.5. Print all keys (joined by comma) and all values (joined by comma).
exercise.starterCode:
const prices: Record<string, number> = {
  apple: 1.5,
  banana: 0.75,
  cherry: 2.0
}

prices["date"] = ___

console.log(Object.keys(prices).join(","))
console.log(Object.values(prices).join(","))

validation.message: Keys and values printed as comma-separated lists
Use Record type and Object utility methods

exercise.hint: Add date with price 3.5. Object.keys returns an array of keys, Object.values returns values.

sections.title: Iterating maps
sections.explanation: Map provides `forEach`, `keys()`, `values()`, and `entries()` for iteration. In interviews, you often iterate to find max/min frequency, collect results, or build output. Destructuring in for-of loops keeps the code clean.

exercise.prompt: Given a Map with entries ["a",3], ["b",7], ["c",1], ["d",7], find all keys with the maximum value. Collect them in an array and print the sorted result joined by comma.

validation.message: Both b and d have the max value of 7
Use destructuring iteration over the Map

exercise.hint: First pass finds maxVal (7). Second pass collects all keys where val === maxVal.
